title: Racosperma mangium
intro: Racosperma mangium, also known as the Australian blackwood or mangium, is a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, native to Australia and Southeast Asia. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering potential health benefits such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities, which may help to alleviate conditions such as fever, respiratory infections, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Racosperma mangium include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and its most common herbal preparations include a decoction of the leaves and bark, which can be used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and digestive problems.
taxonomy: Racosperma mangium is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae. It is native to northern Australia and New Guinea, and has been widely introduced as an ornamental plant. The classification of Racosperma mangium has undergone changes, with some sources still referring to it as Acacia mangium. This species is often used as a shade tree in plantations and gardens. Racosperma mangium is also known for its ability to fix nitrogen in the soil through its symbiotic relationship with certain bacteria.

side_effects: Racosperma mangium, also known as the Australian blackwood, can have several side effects due to its toxic compounds and oils. Ingestion of the plant can cause gastrointestinal issues, nausea, and vomiting in humans and animals. The sap of the plant can also cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
